channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Obama Center’s Two Sides: A Lovely Park and a Forbidding Tower
item.description: In Chicago, the $850 million Obama Presidential Center aims to remake a neighborhood with a 19.3-acre community hub and a brooding 225-foot museum.
item.dc:creator: Michael Kimmelman and Lyndon French
item.category: Obama, Barack H, Presidential Library and Museum
item.media:credit: Lyndon French for The New York Times

item.title: Shannon Elizabeth Reflects on Being the Y2K Era’s ‘Hot Girl’
item.description: Shannon Elizabeth helped define blockbusters like “American Pie” and cult favorites like “Jay and Silent Bob Strike Back.” Now, as she enters a new public chapter, she reflects on the era.
item.dc:creator: Maya Salam
item.category: Jay and Silent Bob Strike Back (Movie)
item.media:credit: Thea Traff for The New York Times

item.title: ‘Amadeus’ and Beyond: Where to Watch Movies About Composers
item.description: The latest adaptation of Peter Shaffer’s “Amadeus” joins a rich lineage of fantastical and extravagant musical biopics.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone

item.title: Movie Highlights at the Tribeca Festival, Now 25
item.description: The standouts from this year’s lineup include films about a cultlike theater troupe and an experimental dance residency.
item.dc:creator: Natalia Winkelman
item.category: I Spy With My Little Eye (Movie)
item.media:credit: Vincent Rosenblatt
item.media:description: Duda Santos in “Funk,” one of the standout films at the Tribeca Festival.

item.title: The Dark Side of Paul McCartney
item.description: Listen to “Eleanor Rigby,” “Daytime Nightime Suffering” and more tracks that hint at his gloomier instincts.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz
item.media:credit: John Sotomayor/The New York Times
item.media:description: Paul McCartney performing with Wings in a 1976 concert.

item.title: New Federal Guidelines Threaten Almost Half of Graduate Arts Programs
item.description: Many musicians, filmmakers and artists earn less than the Education Department’s proposed guidelines for alumni, imperiling federal aid for students in those programs.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small
item.media:credit: Lucy Lu for The New York Times
item.media:description: The master’s degree program in museum studies at Harvard University is among those that would fail proposed federal guidelines examining how much alumni make after graduation.

item.title: Ballet Tech Gave Them Dance. Now They Give Back.
item.description: Kids Dance, from the New York Public School for Dance, is debuting a work featuring alumni. That’s not the only way former students are involved with the school.
item.dc:creator: Brian Seibert
item.media:credit: Kristina Dittmar for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ballet Tech students, from left, Virginie Wallace, Memphis Haas, Amelia Chan, Coral Torres-Hara and Nicholas Begun (an alum). Front, Leo Patel.

item.title: Was Churchill a Serious Artist? This Exhibition Says, ‘Yes.’
item.description: In the first major British retrospective for over 60 years, a London museum seeks to recast the wartime leader as a painter with emotional depth.
item.dc:creator: Leo Sands
item.media:credit: Churchill Archives Centre
item.media:description: A colorized photo of Sir Winston Churchill painting in Belgium in 1946.

item.title: ‘Jerome’ Review: Darkness Swallows a Three-Way Romance
item.description: John J. Caswell’s triangular romance set in the early 1990s speaks to us from the smoking psychic caldera left by AIDS.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw

item.title: MoMA Names Makeda Best as New Photography Chief
item.description: Makeda Best, a deputy director of the Oakland Museum of California and a photographer, will lead the department.
item.dc:creator: Jason Farago

item.title: 17 Plays (and 3 Festivals) to Jazz Up Your June
item.description: Billy Porter, Wayne Brady, Sting and Suzan-Lori Parks are all slated to star on Off Broadway stages this month.
item.dc:creator: Laura Collins-Hughes
item.category: Heated Rivalry: The Unauthorized Musical Parody (Play)

item.title: Martin Scorsese Is Embracing A.I.
item.description: In a sign of Hollywood’s softening stance on artificial intelligence, the cinema icon is backing Black Forest Labs, an image and video generation start-up.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes
item.media:credit: Philip Montgomery for The New York Times
item.media:description: Martin Scorsese signed on last year as a partner and an adviser to Black Forest Labs, a fast-rising A.I. venture.

item.title: Sam Levinson on Why ‘Euphoria’ Had to End This Way. For Good.
item.description: In an exclusive interview, Sam Levinson explained why he felt it was time to bring the series to a close and responded to its detractors.
item.dc:creator: Joe Coscarelli, Jon Caramanica and Caroline Tompkins
item.media:credit: Caroline Tompkins for The New York Times

item.title: In the ‘Euphoria’ Finale, the HBO Show Goes Out With a Whimper
item.description: In the end, a show defined by its excesses seemed to be straining for a redemption that it didn’t need.
item.dc:creator: Mike Hale
item.media:credit: Eddy Chen/HBO
item.media:description: Rue (Zendaya) has been the guide and moral center of “Euphoria” from the beginning.

item.title: Late Night Wants Trump to Give Peace Talks a Chance
item.description: “Oh, I’m sorry, is wrapping up the war you started not interesting enough?” Michael Kosta said on Tuesday, after the president called negotiations with Iran “boring.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)
The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: Peabo Bryson, Soul Singer Known as the Voice of Love, Dies at 75
item.description: He was admired for impassioned duets with singers like Roberta Flack and Celine Dion and for the Disney hits “Beauty and the Beast” and “A Whole New World.”
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:credit: Steve Grayson/WireImage, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Peabo Bryson in 2005. His songs, which dealt with complex relationships and passionate love, dominated the soul and R&B charts for nearly two decades.

item.title: Marcia Lucas, Oscar-Winning ‘Star Wars’ Editor, Dies at 80
item.description: A close collaborator with George Lucas, her first husband, she was “the warmth and the heart” of his early films, the actor Mark Hamill said.
item.dc:creator: Alex Traub
item.media:credit: Associated Press
item.media:description: Marcia Lucas in 1978 with the Academy Award she won for her work on the first “Star Wars” movie, directed by her husband, George Lucas, right. After they divorced, she left the movie business.

item.title: The Best Movies and TV Shows Coming to Netflix in June
item.description: A World Cup comedy and a new film by Julian Schnabel are among this month’s most promising titles.
item.dc:creator: Noel Murray
item.media:credit: ITHOD Productions Ltd.
item.media:description: Oscar Isaac plays both the Italian poet Dante Alighieri and the novelist Nick Tosches in the film adaptation “In the Hand of Dante.”

item.title: Late Night Updates America on the Lineup for Its 250th Birthday Bash
item.description: “Instead of music, the entertainment will be an 80-year-old man yelling about windmills,” Jimmy Kimmel said after President Trump said he would headline a summer concert series.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)
The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: Andy Halliday, a Star of ‘Vampire Lesbians of Sodom,’ Dies at 73
item.description: A beloved member of Charles Busch’s Theater in Limbo repertory, he had an irrepressibly comic stage presence that masked a shy, tender disposition.
item.dc:creator: Penelope Green

item.title: The Best Movies and TV Shows Coming to Disney+, Amazon, AMC+, Apple TV and More in June
item.description: “The Vampire Lestat,” “Hoppers” and “Not Suitable for Work” arrive and “The Bear” returns.
item.dc:creator: Noel Murray
item.category: Bring Me the Beauties: A Model Cult (TV Program)

item.title: Tribeca Festival 25th Anniversary: An Interview With Robert De Niro, Jane Rosenthal, Rebecca Glashow
item.description: Robert De Niro and Jane Rosenthal, who founded the event, and Rebecca Glashow, the new leader of Tribeca Enterprises, reflected on the festival’s legacy and ambitions.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr

item.title: Edward Burns on What It’s like to Be a Tribeca Festival Veteran
item.description: The filmmaker, who is screening his ninth film at the festival, talked about the importance of Tribeca and what he has learned about sustaining an independent filmmaking career.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Vora
item.media:credit: George Etheredge for The New York Times
item.media:description: The filmmaker and actor Edward Burns returns to the Tribeca Festival with his first film shot and produced abroad.

item.title: Tribeca Festival: Andre Holland’s Ability to Connect to Audiences Is on Display
item.description: The actor’s ability to connect to roles is on display in two films, “The Revisionist” and “They Fight,” premiering at this year’s Tribeca Festival.
item.dc:creator: Nicolas Rapold
item.media:credit: Audra Melton for The New York Times
item.media:description: Andre Holland in Marietta, Ga. He is starring in two films premiering in this year’s Tribeca Festival.

item.title: At Tribeca, a New Look at an ’80s Thriller About New York’s Gay Underground
item.description: The troubled legacy of “Cruising” is the subject of “Mineshaft: The Cruising Murders,” a documentary premiering at the Tribeca Festival.
item.dc:creator: Erik Piepenburg

item.title: Azniv Korkejian on Bedouine’s ‘Neon Summer Skin’
item.description: Azniv Korkejian, the recording artist known as Bedouine, turns her own nomadic history and family survival into comforting, classic folk.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz
item.media:credit: Chantal Anderson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Azniv Korkejian, who records as Bedouine, makes music that recalls the folk revival of the 1960s and ’70s.

item.title: 33 Fun Things to Do in N.Y.C. in June
item.description: This month brings an improv all-nighter, James Taylor, “Fraggle Rock” and Puerto Rican pride.
item.dc:creator: Erik Piepenburg
item.category: Hostos Center for the Arts & Culture (Bronx, NY)
Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Timothy A. Clary/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: A participant in last year’s Mermaid Parade at Coney Island. It returns on June 20.

item.title: ‘Awake and Sing!’ Heads to Broadway With Danny Burstein and Jessica Hecht
item.description: Danny Burstein, Jessica Hecht and Jeremy Shamos will star in the Manhattan Theater Club production of Clifford Odets’s 1935 play.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:credit: Angela Weiss/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images, Dia Dipasupil/Getty Images, Dia Dipasupil/Getty Images
item.media:description: From left: Danny Burstein, Jessica Hecht and Jeremy Shamos. Hecht, a three-time Tony nominee, will play Bessie Berger, the family matriarch of “Awake and Sing!”

item.title: Hoyt Richards, Former Model, on ‘Bring Me The Beauties’ and How to Escape a Cult
item.description: Richards, a globe-trotting former model, discusses “Bring Me the Beauties: A Model Cult,” a new three-part docuseries on HBO.
item.dc:creator: Dina Gachman
item.category: Bring Me the Beauties: A Model Cult (TV Program)

item.title: Mark and Joaquin Consuelos, Father and Son, Make Broadway Debuts Together
item.description: This spring the talk-show host and his youngest child made simultaneous debuts, three weeks and eight blocks apart.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson and OK McCausland
item.media:description: “I’m already sad that it’s going to be over,” Joaquin Consuelos said. “And now I want to do everything. I don’t want to limit myself.”

item.title: What’s On in London Theaters This Summer
item.description: Catherine Tate, Greek classics and plenty in between — here’s our selection of West End productions for Londoners and visitors to check out over the coming months.
item.dc:creator: Matt Wolf

item.title: 9 Comic Books and Graphic Novels to Celebrate Pride Month
item.description: Historical chronicles and flights of fancy, all with L.G.B.T.Q. protagonists, arrive starting in June.
item.dc:creator: George Gene Gustines
item.category: Wiccan and Hulkling: Raid of Ultron (Book)
Justice League: Dream Girls A DC Pride Event (Book)
Bitter Pill: Randy Shilts and the Dawn of the HIV/AIDS Epidemic (Book)
item.media:credit: Brandt & Stein/DC

item.title: 28 New Books to Read in June: Ann Patchett, Maggie O’Farrell, Daniel Kraus and More
item.description: Novels by Ann Patchett, Maggie O’Farrell and Dave Eggers; memoirs by Jill Biden and Laverne Cox; sci-fi adventures by a Pulitzer Prize winner; and more.
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Joana Avillez Revisits Her Waterfront Childhood
item.description: Joana Avillez took six years to illustrate a new edition of Joseph Mitchell’s “The Bottom of the Harbor,” which captures the salty New York neighborhood of her youth.
item.dc:creator: Alex Vadukul
item.media:credit: Elias Williams for The New York Times
item.media:description: Joana Avillez poses for a portrait not far from the building where she grew up, near the South Street Seaport in Lower Manhattan.

item.title: ‘Cape Fear’ and 7 More Shows to Watch on TV This Week
item.description: A new Apple TV series premieres, and the 79th Tony Awards ceremony will be broadcast live.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez
item.media:credit: Courtesy of Apple TV
item.media:description: Patrick Wilson and Amy Adams in “Cape Fear.”

item.title: ‘And Then the Rodeo Burned Down’ Review: Tenderness as Transgression
item.description: In their Off Broadway debut at Ars Nova, Xhloe and Natasha play two rodeo clowns, until the lights go out and the show takes a turn.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.category: And Then the Rodeo Burned Down (Play)

item.title: Vasana Montgomery, ‘Love Island USA’ Contestant, Dismissed After Using Racial Slur
item.description: Last season, two contestants were also kicked off the reality show when videos showing them using racial slurs surfaced during filming.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:credit: via Peacock
item.media:description: Two videos of Vasana Montgomery, 25, using a racial slur surfaced on social media last week.

item.title: Young Moviegoers Power ‘Backrooms’ to $82 Million in Ticket Sales
item.description: The horror film, which cost $10 million and came from a 20-year-old filmmaker, added to evidence that young people will go to theaters for the right offering.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes

item.title: ‘Othello’ Review: Wendell Pierce Steps Into Iago’s Twisted Web
item.description: This production in the nation’s capital, with an enticingly opaque Iago, attempts to make Shakespeare’s tragedy relevant to our age of conspiracies.
item.dc:creator: Rhoda Feng

item.title: A Festival of Black Culture With a ’90s Vibe
item.description: The brothers behind the London party organizers Recess decamped to a seaside amusement park for a two-day event channeling an earlier era of Black British nightlife.
item.dc:creator: Kadish Morris and Sophie Stafford
item.media:credit: Sophie Stafford for The New York Times

item.title: Trump Urges Canceling Freedom 250 Concerts After Artists Drop Out
item.description: The president had earlier said on social media that he should take the place of “these highly paid, Third Rate ‘Artists,’” saying he gets “larger audiences than Elvis.”
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:credit: Bryan Anselm for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump at an event in Suffern, N.Y., last week.

item.title: A Judge Ordered Trump’s Name to be Removed From the Kennedy Center. What Happens Next?
item.description: A federal judge ordered the Kennedy Center to take President Trump’s name off the building. What happens next?
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague and Julia Jacobs
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump, the chairman of the Kennedy Center, wrote on social media that “we are going to be working with Congress to transfer this failing Institution back to them.”

item.title: Ariana Grande, Ravyn Lenae: 7 Songs We’re Talking About This Week
item.description: Ariana Grande moves back into pop, Iceage crashes into the cosmos, Ravyn Lenae channels the 1980s and more.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: After a couple of years spent immersed in “Wicked” world, Ariana Grande returns to pop with her newest single.

item.title: ‘Spider-Noir’ Review: Your Friendly Neighborhood Nicolas Cage
item.description: An often unbridled actor gets to play a mutant in a mash-up of superheroes and film noir on Amazon Prime Video.
item.dc:creator: Mike Hale

item.title: 50 Years of Skateboarding at London’s Southbank Undercroft
item.description: Skateboarders make pilgrimages from around the world to a “found” London space that a grass-roots campaign saved from redevelopment.
item.dc:creator: Eleanor Stanford and Alice Zoo
item.media:credit: Alice Zoo for The New York Times

item.title: On ‘Euphoria’ and Elsewhere, TV Is Having a Sex Cam Spring
item.description: Virtual sex work is nothing new on TV. But this year it is more visible, central to series like “Euphoria,” “Margo’s Got Money Troubles” and “Maximum Pleasure Guaranteed.”
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski

item.title: 7 Podcasts About the Joys of Bird-Watching
item.description: These shows offer expert advice and fun facts that will help both novices and enthusiasts get the most out of tuning into the winged world.
item.dc:creator: Emma Dibdin
item.category: Get Birding With Sean Bean (Radio Program)
Life List: A Birding Podcast (Radio Program)
item.media:credit: Irene Rinaldi

item.title: Ginger Minj Knows You Can Never Go Wrong With Rhinestones
item.description: “I blame Dolly Parton for this in some way,” the “RuPaul’s Drag Race” star said, “but it’s just right, it’s fun and it’s eye-catching.”
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.category: RuPaul's Drag Race All Stars (TV Program)
item.media:credit: Santiago Felipe/Getty Images for Mtv

item.title: Claude Bessy, Who Ruled Paris Opera Ballet School, Dies at 93
item.description: A tall, commanding ballerina, she led one of the world’s top dance institutions for decades, though her rigorous methods eventually drew criticism.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter
item.media:credit: Roger Viollet, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Claude Bessy in 1957.

item.title: Kennedy Center Must Remove Trump’s Name From Building, Judge Orders
item.description: In an incensed social media post, President Trump suggested that the ruling might prompt him to cast the center aside after more than a year at its helm.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs and Zach Montague
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: “Congress gave the Kennedy Center its name, and only Congress can change it,” Judge Christopher R. Cooper of the Federal District Court in Washington wrote in a ruling on Friday.

item.title: Overlooked No More: Nancy Sheung, Whose Camera Captured Women on Their Own Terms
item.description: In 1960s Hong Kong, she used photography to portray women as bold, self-possessed and unconstrained by traditional expectations.
item.dc:creator: Stella Raine Chu

item.title: Randall Bourscheidt, Wide-Ranging Arts Advocate, Dies at 81
item.description: He helped triple New York City’s arts budget, led research to promote cultural funding and supported artists with H.I.V. and AIDS.
item.dc:creator: Tim Page
item.media:credit: Will Ragozzino/Patrick McMullan, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Randall Bourscheidt speaking at a 2010 event for the Alliance for the Arts, the advocacy group he led from 1989 to 2010.

item.title: Five International Movies to Stream Now
item.description: This month’s picks include an Argentine comedy based on a real-life incident, a queer story set in 1980s Chile, a documentary about the aftermath of the Troubles in Belfast and more.
item.dc:creator: Devika Girish
item.category: The Mysterious Gaze of the Flamingo (Movie)

item.title: Review: ‘Girls Girls Chance Chance Music Music’ Loves a Good Riff
item.description: In Eisa Davis’s new play with live music, at the Vineyard Theater, gifted teenagers find their own rhythms at a summer music program.
item.dc:creator: Laura Collins-Hughes
item.category: Girls Girls Chance Chance Music Music (Play)

item.title: Olivia Rodrigo Discusses the Inspiration Behind Her New Album
item.description: The punk-leaning pop star tapped the vibrancy of 1980s new wave and the sentiment of “Sex and the City” on her new album “You Seem Pretty Sad For a Girl So in Love.”
item.dc:creator: Jon Caramanica, Joe Coscarelli and Caroline Tompkins
item.category: You Seem Pretty Sad For a Girl So in Love (Album)
item.media:credit: Caroline Tompkins for The New York Times

item.title: When a Reluctant Songwriting Star Found Refuge on a New York Stage
item.description: A reissue chronicles the Joe’s Pub performances of Allen Toussaint, who wrote “Working in the Coal Mine” and other hits, after Hurricane Katrina forced him out of New Orleans.
item.dc:creator: Steve Knopper
item.media:credit: Nicole Bengiveno/The New York Times
item.media:description: “He really preferred to stay in the studio and never be the focal point,” Clarence Reginald Toussaint said of his father. “That was never his objective.”

item.title: 9 New Movies Our Critics Are Talking About This Week
item.description: Whether you’re a casual moviegoer or an avid buff, our reviewers think these films are worth knowing about.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: ‘Hacks’ Was Always a Love Story
item.description: In its finale, this comedy about comedy circled back to the romance of creative partnership and the saving power of laughs.
item.dc:creator: James Poniewozik

item.title: NYC Pride Events 2026: Where to Celebrate
item.description: Get ready for drag shows, marches, musicals, parades and a “Heated Rivalry” parody.
item.dc:creator: Erik Piepenburg
item.media:credit: Natalie Keyssar for The New York Times
item.media:description: Members of the Queer Big Apple Corps marching band at last year’s Pride March in Manhattan. It returns on June 28, starting at noon.

item.title: In ‘Fallen Angels,’ Kelli O’Hara and Rose Byrne Get Laughs Getting Sloshed
item.description: The Tony-nominated stars of “Fallen Angels” demonstrate how to act drunk onstage and have a hilarious hangover.
item.dc:creator: Jesse Green and Mohamed Sadek
item.media:credit: Mohamed Sadek for The New York Times

item.title: This Dancer Is Helping to Expand What a Ballet Body Can Be
item.description: Olivia Book of Ballet West is one of the first professional ballet dancers to have a limb difference.
item.dc:creator: Sophie Bress
item.media:credit: Ian Willms for The New York Times
item.media:description: Olivia Book, who is from Grimsby, Ontario, on the shore of Lake Ontario.
